items.q: Which of the following is a fundamental particle: proton, electron, or neutron?
items.correct: Only the electron — protons and neutrons are made of quarks.
items.misconception: All three (proton, electron, neutron) are fundamental particles that cannot be broken down.
items.random_wrong: Only the proton is fundamental; electrons and neutrons are composite.
items.why: The atomic model is introduced with proton/neutron/electron as building blocks, so students don't update when quarks appear in later courses.

items.q: When does an electron behave as a wave?
items.correct: In experiments that probe wave properties (e.g. diffraction through a crystal); in particle-like experiments it behaves as a particle.
items.misconception: Electrons are always waves and only look like particles at low energy.
items.random_wrong: Electrons are sometimes waves and sometimes particles depending on the temperature of the apparatus.
items.why: Students seek a single 'true' description; the modern view is that the experimental context selects which behaviour is observed.

items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.
Correct answer: A quark and an antiquark. “3 quarks” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Total charge of uud combination (in units of e)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
